title: ESHKOL_VALUE_HANDLE lifecycle states + runtime warn-mode linear check
goal: Introduce a new tagged-value subtype ESHKOL_VALUE_HANDLE that wraps resources with explicit lifecycle states {open, borrowed, closed}. File ports, SQLite handles, subprocess pids all become handles. Runtime warns (not errors) on double-close or use-after-close for v1.3; compile-time enforcement waits for v1.9 dependent linear types. Pre-builds the v1.4 socket-safety story.
next_actions: Define HEAP_SUBTYPE_HANDLE (new subtype value) in inc/eshkol/core/runtime.h alongside the other subtypes.

Define struct eshkol_handle_t { kind: HANDLE_KIND_FILE_PORT|HANDLE_KIND_SQLITE|HANDLE_KIND_SUBPROCESS|HANDLE_KIND_SOCKET (reserved), state: STATE_OPEN|STATE_BORROWED|STATE_CLOSED, native_handle: void*, source_span: span_t } in inc/eshkol/core/handle.h.

lib/core/runtime_handle.cpp: handle-make, handle-borrow (transitions open→borrowed), handle-release (borrowed→open), handle-close (open→closed), handle-state, handle-kind. Each transition warns on illegal state via eshkol_warn_at with the source span.
Refactor file ports (lib/core/runtime_string_ports_hosted.cpp), SQLite handles (lib/core/agent_sqlite.c), and subprocess pids (lib/core/agent_subprocess.c) to wrap their native resources in handles.
Add tests/v1_3_edge_cases/handle_lifecycle_test.esk: open/close round-trip, double-close warns, use-after-close warns, borrow/release pairing.
acceptance: Double-close of a file port emits a warning containing the close site source span.
Use-after-close of a SQLite statement emits a warning.
Existing v1.2 file/SQLite/subprocess code keeps working unchanged (no API-breaking behavior changes; warnings are advisory).
v1_4_payoff: When v1.4 introduces sockets, they slot in as HANDLE_KIND_SOCKET with the same lifecycle discipline. No new infrastructure needed.
